symbols: lush garden — the original paradise created by God for humanity, a place of perfect harmony
solitary figure by water — the first human in an unspoiled world, reflecting the state of original innocence
abundant vegetation — the fecundity of God's creation before the Fall
water — life-giving source flowing through Eden (Gen 2:10), symbol of God's sustaining grace
distant misty landscape — the vast, unbounded goodness of the created order
description: A sepia-toned landscape showing a luxuriant garden with towering trees, dense foliage, and tropical palms framing the composition. At the center, a solitary nude figure stands at the edge of a pool or stream, bending slightly forward. The garden recedes into a misty, expansive valley with distant trees, gentle hills, and what appears to be a fountain or waterfall at the far right. Hanging vines and broad-leaved plants fill the foreground. The overall tone is warm sepia with soft tonal gradations suggesting hazy, golden light.
theological_description: John Martin's depiction of Eden visualizes the primordial paradise of Genesis 2:8-15, the garden God planted eastward in Eden where He placed the man He had formed. The overwhelming abundance of vegetation and water conveys the goodness of creation — 'God saw everything that He had made, and behold, it was very good' (Gen 1:31). The solitary figure evokes the moment before the creation of Eve, or perhaps the first experience of wonder at the created order. Patristic writers from Ephrem the Syrian to Augustine saw Eden as both a literal place and a symbol of the soul in communion with God. The garden's beauty carries an undertone of fragility: this is paradise before the Fall, a state that will be lost through disobedience but restored eschatologically in the new creation (Rev 22:1-2), where the river of life and the tree of life reappear.

scripture_refs.reason: God plants the Garden of Eden and places humanity within it
God sees all that He has made and declares it very good
The eschatological restoration of paradise with the river and tree of life
